content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Henry S. Barbosa 
Barbosa &. Vera 
Los Angeles Corporate Center 
November 15, 1988 
1000 Corporate Center Dr., Ste. 350 
Monterey Park, CA 
91754 
Re: 
88-434 
Dear Mr. Barbosa: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on November 14, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to the information needed. 
If your request is 
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we 
can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. 
Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
JP:plh 
428 J Street Suite 800
•
P ()
D
~~ry truly yours, 
,/' 
X 
/.~\//~/ 
( 
~eanne Pritchard 
jChief 
Technical Assistance 
Division 
and Analysis 


HENRY S. BARBOSA 
RONALD T. VERA 
RICHARD J. MORILLO 
DOUGLAS D. BARNES 
GONZALO P. CURIEL 
LYNNE S. BASSIS 
JOSEFINA J. JARAMILLO 
BARBOSA S VERA 
ATTORNEYS AT LAW 
LOS ANGELES CORPORATE CENTER 
SUITE 350 
1000 CORPORATE CENTER DRIVE 
MONTEREY PARK, CALIFORNIA 91754 
TELEPHONE (213) 263 - 5199 
FACSIMILE (213) 263-0683 
November 10, 1988 
Mr. Kevin Braaten-Moen 
Political Reform Consultant 
Fair Political Practices commission 
428 "J" street, Suite 700 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Re: 
city of Lynwood 
Dear Mr. Braaten-Moen: 
LOS ANGELES OFFICE 
655 SOUTH HOPE STREET 
SUITE 1300 
LOS ANGELES, CA 90017 
(213) 688-7927 
Thank you for taking the time on November 8, 
1988 to 
discuss with me two matters of concern to the city of Lynwood 
relative to the application of Prop 73. 
As to the first item, I understand from our conversation 
that it is permissible in the view of the Commission that the 
ci ty of Lynwood mail Christmas greetings in the name of the 
ci ty of Lynwood so long as the names of individual elected 
officials are not printed. 
The second item involves a question on behalf of the city 
of Lynwood and the Lynwood Information Inc. 
As I indicated in 
our conversation, 
Lynwood Information Inc. is a non-profit 
corporation organized by the city of Lynwood to promote public 
relations on behalf of the city and disseminate information to 
the public. 
Our specific question is as follows: 
Can Lynwood 
Information 
Inc., 
solicit 
and 
subscribe 
for 
advertisers 
through, for example, the roster of the Chamber of Commerce, 
and based upon the revenues derived from such advertising 
sUbscriptions operate outside the restrictions of Prop 73 so as 
to permit the city Council members to be identified in such 
publication? 


Mr. Kevin Braaten-Moen 
Re: 
city of Lynwood 
November 10, 1988 
Page Two 
Your prompt written response to this inquiry will be very 
much 
appreciated. 
If there is any 
further question or 
information I 
can provide in connection with this inquiry, 
please do not hesitate to call upon me. 
HSB:nr 1105 
cc: 
Charles Gomez, 
City Manager 
ours, 
Barbosa, 
Attorney 
of Lynwood

sections.question: Can Lynwood Information Inc., a non-profit corporation organized by the city of Lynwood to promote public relations and disseminate information, solicit and subscribe for advertisers (for example, through the Chamber of Commerce roster) and based upon the revenues derived from such advertising subscriptions operate outside the restrictions of Prop 73 so as to permit the city Council members to be identified in such publication?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city-organized non-profit corporation can solicit advertising subscriptions and use those revenues to operate a publication that identifies city council members outside of Proposition 73's restrictions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (from attorney) and an acknowledgment receipt from FPPC dated November 15, 1988. The FPPC receipt confirms this is an informal advice request. No formal Q/C sections in the incoming letter; question extracted from narrative text. The FPPC response letter indicates this is classified as document 88-434 but does not contain the actual advice response.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Can Lynwood Information Inc., a non-profit corporation organized by the city of Lynwood to promote public relations and disseminate information, solicit and subscribe for advertisers (for example, through the Chamber of Commerce roster) and based upon the revenues derived from such advertising subscriptions operate outside the restrictions of Prop 73 so as to permit the city Council members to be identified in such publication?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from Barbosa & Vera attorneys to the FPPC dated November 10, 1988, seeking written advice on behalf of the City of Lynwood regarding Proposition 73 compliance. The letter includes two questions: whether the city can mail Christmas greetings without printing individual officials' names, and whether Lynwood Information Inc. can operate a publication identifying council members if funded through advertising subscriptions.
